title: The Creature
excerpt: Book 1 of the 4Corner Collection. Coming-of-age fiction following Jay — early-morning physics projects, the long route past an old playground, and a recurring caramel nightmare he can't shake. Quiet and introspective, with an undercurrent of unease. (Chapter 26 in progress.)
content:
Jay was playing in pools filled with caramel, surrounded by cities made of chocolate and marshmallows. Diving into his pool he slurps up as much caramel as he can fill into his mouth. He slowly swallows all of it, as he begins to sink deeper and deeper in the confectionary filled pool. He continues to sink, eating candy after candy, only consuming more as he descends. Through the endless abyss of sugary delight, floating glittery unicorns made of marshmallows, and many hundreds of chocolate bunnies all floating along with him. As he keeps on consuming these sweets, his eyes grow heavy, and he struggles to see in the caramel around him. He was able to breathe somehow, which came to his attention rather late, as he found that nothing made any sense. As he keeps sinking, his chest tightens as the pressure of the caramel begins to weigh down upon him. He coughs, and gasps for air as his lungs are filled with heavy syrup. His eyes jolt open as he begins to struggle and flail around, but his efforts were to no avail as he continued to sink, until he hit the bottom, where he promptly woke up from this interesting dream of his. He sat upright in his bed, looking around the room wildly.

About: A slice-of-life story where mundane details carry the emotional weight. Jay lives with his mom, his often-absent dad, and his sharp-tongued younger sister Mia. He works on physics projects before school, listens to Daft Punk, and takes the long way through an old playground to meet his closest friend Jenny — who wears a backpack covered in enamel pins, many gifted by him, and knows his caramel nightmare by name. Themes: stress and escapism, family distance, teenage friendship, and the gap between an inner life and an outward calm. Originally written on Discord, now expanded; new characters Abby and Suki arrive in Chapter 26.

title: Continuity
excerpt: Book 2 of the 4Corner Collection. A grounded, funny high-school story — coding class, McDonald's runs, a sleepy English teacher who springs surprise essays — where the narrator accidentally activates a hidden power and badly misunderstands how it works.
content:
Chocolate appeared on my desk again. No note, no warning — just there, between my notebook and the edge where the wood is worn smooth. Across the room my best friend was already asleep, the way he always is by second period, right up until a marker bounced off his forehead. Coding class. We were supposed to be writing loops. I was playing Minecraft. That's the part they caught me for — not the other thing, the thing I'd done by accident that morning that I still didn't understand. By lunch we were doing the McDonald's run like nothing was different. By the end of the day I was sitting in the principal's office, and the chocolate was still in my pocket.

About: Contemporary fiction with supernatural elements, set in a recognizable high school (CSA coding class, a sleepy English teacher named Johnathan). It starts light and comedic and is built to darken as the stakes rise: the narrator keeps his misunderstanding of the power to himself, escalates its use because it's subtle and controllable, and eventually goes too far — doing something he fully regrets, at which point the power stops working.

title: Loss of Power
excerpt: Book 3 of the 4Corner Collection. Sci-fi set roughly 800 years from now, where screens called 'eyesets' plug directly into your eyes. A quiet collector keeps chasing the old analog things the world has moved on from.
content:
I almost made it to my dorm door before the light found me. The coffee machine — antique, heavy, beautiful in the way only useless old things are — was gone from my hands before I understood the light was a confiscation and not just a glare. I went back to my bunk and powered on my eyeset out of habit. News I didn't want, scores I didn't care about, knowledge I hadn't asked for, painted across the inside of my eyes where no one else could see it. I watched it for a while. Then I sighed, switched it off, and looked at the rest of my collection: shelves of devices, limited editions and vintage models, all of them silent, none of them the thing I actually wanted.

About: A contemplative, slightly melancholy first-person story. Eyesets are the primary information interface of this future; old analog technology is rare and stored in restricted 'ancient-tech rooms.' The narrator is a collector and observer who finds meaning in old things. Themes: technology and human connection, nostalgia, institutional control, and what gets preserved versus discarded.

title: Untitled (with Nathan)
excerpt: Book 4 of the 4Corner Collection, co-written with Nathan. A psychological thriller / horror with surreal, dreamlike elements — Bob's dreams are haunted by a demon, and the line between a facility and a dream keeps blurring.
content:
He barricaded the door with the cabinet and gripped the bat he'd made himself — nails driven through the head, never once used. On the other side his brother's voice begged him to open it. He didn't. It wasn't his brother; he was sure of that, as sure as he'd ever been of anything, and being sure was the only thing keeping his hands from the handle. The door opened on its own.

About: Bob is a college student carrying childhood trauma; he moved away from the home where most of it originates. The chapters alternate between mundane school life — waking up a little later each day, disengaged in class, doodling and falling asleep — and something stranger beneath it: a hallway of steel-mesh doors and flashing lights, a dining room of seated figures beckoning him, a neatly dressed girl in a pink dress who 'looked odd.' The world is described simply as 'Dream.' Themes: trauma, paranoia, the unreliability of safety, and identity.

title: A Breakup Letter to 2025
excerpt: A short experimental piece — a breakup letter addressed to a year, written in the shape of famous historical correspondence (Burton/Taylor, Hemingway/Kurowsky, Nin/Baldwin, Kahlo/Rivera). Reflective and light.
content:
Dear 2025, we both knew it wasn't going to last twelve months. You were thrilling and exhausting in equal measure — and I think we'd both agree it's time. This is me, writing it down the way the great letter-writers did, so it counts.

About: An AP English assignment reimagined as a personal essay — a farewell to a year done in the register of historical love letters, balancing humor with genuine reflection.

title: Gatsby, Confessional
excerpt: An in-character monologue and literary analysis built around The Great Gatsby (Chapter 6) — illusion versus reality, the American Dream, and obsession, written for performance.
content:
Old sport — let me tell you how a man builds a self out of nothing but want. I analyze the chapter and then I become him, because that's the only honest way to read someone who was never quite real to begin with.

About: An AP English piece pairing close literary analysis of Gatsby's Chapter 6 with a first-person dramatic monologue performed in character. Themes: illusion vs. reality, the American Dream, and obsession.

title: On Preserving What's Left
excerpt: A persuasive essay (co-written with Ethan and Aadi) arguing for lowering the criteria of historical-building preservation programs — on economic, environmental, and cultural grounds.
content:
Tearing a building down is rarely the cheapest option once you count what's lost. We argue that the bar for protecting historic structures is set too high — and that loosening it pays back economically, environmentally, and culturally.

About: An AP English group essay making a structured, evidence-based case for broadening historical-preservation criteria.
